data.title: Melt-Up Ahead?
data.description:
If you had told anyone following financial market trends on April 2 this year that by September just about any asset class under the sun would be basking in positive returns for the year, they would have called you crazy. But the doom and gloom of the “liberation day” tariff announcement quickly dissipated, and since then, the markets have learned to take each and every curve ball thrown their way in stride, shrugging off any longer-term implications while reveling in the joys of a short-term sugar rush. Everything from gold to emerging market and European equities to our own S&P 500 is up double digits, and even the once-jittery bond market has settled into a comfortable place with prices up low to mid-single digits across a spectrum of credit risk. It’s a year for the Pollyannas of the world. And that sugar rush may have a ways to go before it ends.

Party Like It’s 2021

It wasn’t all that long ago that we had a similar dynamic. 2021 was the year of the “everything rally,” the year when the punters of the world united to pump up the prices of dubious stocks like GameStop while loading up on all things crypto and outbidding each other for “unique” digital images of primates in various postures of ennui. The fun back then ended, of course, when the Fed took away the punchbowl of zero-level interest rates and began its extensive monetary tightening program in early 2022.

Interest rates are still high relative to where they were in 2021, but that may be about to change in a way that gives this year’s everything-goes rally another burst of upward movement. The Federal Open Market Committee will meet next week, and investors are all but certain that, come 2:00 pm Eastern time next Wednesday, the Fed funds target rate will drop by 0.25 percent. The FOMC has three more meetings this year (including next week’s), and prediction markets have pegged a rate cut for each of those meetings, having the Fed funds rate end the year 0.75 percent lower than where it is today. Couple that with the frequent impulse for markets to rise during the holiday season, and it’s not hard to see why pundits from a number of corners are furiously raising their estimates for risk-asset performance between now and New Year’s Eve.

Reality Bites Back (Sometime, Probably)

A rationally-minded person might ask why all this good cheer is happening at a time when there doesn’t seem to be much in the way of good news out there. The labor market is in a clear slowdown phase, and this week we learned that the BLS jobs numbers reported for the twelve months between March 2024 and March 2025 were revised down by 911,000 – that’s roughly 75,000 fewer payroll gains over that period than previously reported. Also this week, we saw that inflation is not showing much sign of trending down. Year-on-year core inflation (measured by the Consumer Price Index) is up 3.1 percent. Grocery prices, which don’t show up in the core inflation number but which very much do show up in household budgets, rose by 0.6 percent in the month of August alone. The effect of tariffs as a sales tax on consumers is showing up in an ever-widening array of goods and services.

The stickiness of inflation complicates the Fed’s interest rate calculus, but the central bank has been clear in recent messaging that current concerns are tilted towards the jobs side of its dual mandate. So while the market is probably right that rate cuts are coming, they will be coming with plenty of attendant concerns about future developments. Several FOMC members have expressed confidence that inflation will level off and subside after one or two months of tariff-related increases, so job number one is making sure that employment conditions don’t venture far enough south to push the economy into recession territory. Maybe they’re right – and we can see a world in which markets continue to buy into this worldview for some time to come. But reality has a way of biting back, sooner or later. In our view, this is a time neither for overly defensive pessimism nor excessive optimism.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Boston Beer Is A Reasonably Priced Growth Stock
data.description:
The Boston Beer Company (NYSE:SAM) has dropped in price over the past year and especially over the past five years, and its price has increased only 2% over the past decade. While the company has struggled recently, it is a growth stock. In this piece, I explain what impacted the company, why its outlook is very positive and why I find it to be attractively priced.

What The Boston Beer Company Does

Founded in 1984, the Boston-based company went public in late 1995. Founder Jim Koch, who became CEO again this summer, moved the company beyond beer in 1997 with Hardcore Cider. The company sells a lot of beer, but it does other beverages too. The company operates in one segment, and its filings and news releases don't break out beer sales or other beverages.

The company's website lists 5 brands on its "About Us" page, including beer brands Samuel Adams and Dogfish Head. It also mentions Truly (seltzer that is 5% alcohol, launched in 2016), Twisted Tea (a hard tea drink, launched in 2001) and Angry Orchard (hard cider, launched in 1997). While I don't like that the company does not break down sales by category, I do like that it has moved beyond beer.

Founder Jim Koch serves as CEO and owns a lot of the stock (20% as of 12/31/24)). CFO Diego Reynoso joined the company two years ago and worked previously at Tyson Foods, Constellation Brands and Beam Suntory.

In H1 of this year, the company generated revenue of $1.04 billion, up 4% from the first half of 2024. Gross margin improved from 45.0% to 49.1%. SG&A was boosted by higher advertising spending, growing 8%. Operating income expanded by 34%. EPS, reported at $7.58, grew 40%. For H1, the company generated $128.4 million of operating cash flow and spent $24.2 million on capital expenditures. So, SAM was growing and generating free cash flow.

I am primarily a cannabis industry analyst, but my interest in SAM has little to do with their involvement in the cannabis industry, which is restricted to Canadian beverages. In their 10-K, the word "cannabis" appeared a dozen times. The company in 2021 set up a subsidiary to develop non-alcoholic cannabis beverages, and it first sold Emerald Hour products in 2024. The company does not disclose sales levels of this product, and it has no plans to produce or sell products outside of Canada at this time. In the U.S., cannabis is more of a threat than an opportunity currently.

How the Pandemic Impacted Boston Beer

I first noticed this stock in 2020 when it soared during the pandemic. This move was driven by rapidly growing sales of Truly. I noticed how crowded my grocery store was with those seltzers.

During the pandemic, people were at home, were worried, and could not spend money in a lot of ways to which they had become accustomed. It's not surprising that people drank more alcoholic beverages, including these seltzers. The stock shot up a lot early on in 2020 after the pandemic hit, and it went up throughout the year. It gained 163.1% in 2020, hitting an all-time high near $1350 in April.

Again, I follow the cannabis industry closely, and it saw big demand during the early days of the pandemic too, as did other alcohol companies.

A More Recent Challenge

A move away from drinking alcohol may be weighing on the stock, but typically in weak economies, people do drink more. The economy does appear to be weakening!

More recently, the tariffs have an impact on the company. The company reports that 6% of its revenue is from outside of the U.S. (mainly Canada). In its Q2 press release, the company guided to a total cost of $15-20 million for 2025, which was down from its prior guidance. It estimates that the EPS hit will be $0.96 to $1.28. In Q2, the impact was $3.0 million.

The Boston Beer Outlook Is Promising

SAM has ten analysts covering the company, and, according to Koyfin, the analysts expect revenue to decline in 2025 by 2% to $1.97 billion and then to increase by 1.5% in 2026 to $2.00 billion. EBITDA is forecast to decline 5% in 2025 to $232.7 million and then climb 12% to $260.5 million in 2026. EPS are projected to drop 5% in 2025 to $8.99 and then rise 20% in 2026 to $10.75. The current 2027 projection is $12.41, so the EPS are expanding, though earnings are down a lot from 2021.

SAM Is Cheap

The company has 8.82 million Class A shares and 2.07 million Class B shares as of 7/18. The B shares, owned by CEO and Chairman Koch, are convertible into Class A shares and have voting rights. At the end of Q2, there were 287K options outstanding, but the weighted average exercise price was $327.35. I exclude the options from the fully-diluted share-count, but I add 225K RSUs outstanding to get 11.12 million shares. At $220, this gives SAM a market cap of $2.45 billion. The company has been repurchasing its stock, and spent $99.1 million in H1, paying $236.70 per share on average. On 8/11, it entered into a 10b5-1 plan to repurchase up to $50 million in Q4, which is what they had done for Q3 (announced by SEC filing in May).

The balance sheet at the end of Q2 was very strong, with no debt and total liabilities of $356 million. The ratio of current assets to current liabilities was a healthy 1.8X, with cash of $212 million. At $220, the enterprise value is $2.23 billion.

The company ended Q2 with total equity of $912 million, so the price/book is 2.7X. Reducing the equity for intangibles and goodwill, leaves the price to tangible book value at 3.1X, which seems pretty low for a debt-free and profitable company and offers some downside protection.

The stock currently trades at 25 PE for 2025, but a PE for 2026 of 20.5X. Using enterprise value to projected EBITDA, the stock trades at 8.6X projected EBITDA for 2026. These metrics seem low compared to the history over the past decade:

Compared to peers Anheuser-Busch InBev (BUD), Constellation Brands (STZ) and Diageo (DEO), all of which are larger in market cap, the stock seems attractive. BUD, with a market cap of $114 billion, trades at 15X PE and 9X EV EBITDA. It has a lot of debt. Constellation Brands has a market cap near $25 billion. Its PE is 12X, and its EV/EBITDA is 10.5X. Diageo has a market cap of $56 billion and trades with a forward PE of 15X and an EV/EBITDA of 12X. These three stocks do pay a dividend, while SAM does not. All three of them have negative tangible book value, so SAM looks better protected to the downside.

It Is a Potential Acquisition Target

SAM has been involved with potential acquisitions already, and I think that the stock is cheap enough and the company good enough that it could eventually take place, though my interest in the stock isn't based on this.

Ben Kovler, Founder and Chairman and CEO of Green Thumb Industries (OTCQX:GTBIF), a leading MSO in the cannabis space, sent a letter to Jim Koch in June 2024 suggesting that the two companies merge. The Wall Street Journal published a story about it on June 4th, which was after it had reported a potential acquisition by Suntory that was reported on 5/31. Suntory is interesting, as Kovler's father sold Jim Beam to the company, and SAM's CFO used to work there in 2016 and 2017.

Here is the letter from Kovler to Koch:

I was amused by the GTI inquiry, as I understood how difficult it would be for the NYSE-listed SAM to become an OTC company. I do like the idea a lot of a leading cannabis company merging with a leading alcohol company, but the timing was not and is not right. Both stocks are down a lot since then. Suntory also revealed just before the GTI news that it was not in discussions. I actually owned the stock and sold ahead of this as it had rallied above $300.

There are other companies that could be interested in SAM in my view. Heineken (OTCQX:HEINY) and Molson Coors (TAP) were mentioned in 2024 ahead of the Suntory rumor. TAP has a market cap of $9.6 billion with debt of $6.5 billion, and the stock trades at just 8.7 PE, so it doesn't seem that likely to me. HEINY is bigger and trades at a higher PE, but below SAM. Again, I am not counting on M&A.

Koch became CEO again in August, replacing Michael Spillane, who had been named CEO in April of 2024. Spillane resigned for personal reasons. Koch served previously as CEO from 1984 to 2001. On the last conference call, which preceded this change, Koch spoke and answered several questions. I like that the person running the company owns so much stock and am confident in him that he will look for a good merger if he pursues that strategy.

The SAM Chart Is Promising

While SAM is down in 2025 and down a lot since it peaked in 2021, it has done very well since its IPO, rising 675% in 30 years:

Over the past 10 years, the stock is up only 0.5%, but the growth since the IPO 30 years ago has been 7.1% annualized.

Looking at the more recent action, the stock is trading above the 185.34 low that was set in June, but it is way down from the 395 area in September 2023. There have been a couple of gaps down in trading:

Conclusion

I follow about 90 stocks for personal investment, and SAM is my best idea for GARP investors. I don't own it now, but I have owned it off and on over the past 18 months. I am very bearish on stocks right now. My wife does hold it in her IRA.

The company seems to be growing again after growing too fast during the pandemic and then shrinking. The company has a strong balance sheet, and the stock seems cheap.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best GARP Idea investment competition, which runs through September 23. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: The North West Company: Defensive By Nature, Challenged By Fire And Fiscal Tightening
data.description:
In my last piece on North West Company (TSX:NWC:CA) - a rather unconventional retailer-and-service provider operating mainly in remote communities across Northern and Western Canada, as well as the Caribbean and the Virgin Islands - I landed on a ‘Hold,’ with a price target somewhere in the mid-$40s.

In some ways, the stock still works as a decent Defensive Pick. It throws off a low-to-mid single-digit yield, carries little debt (Net Debt/EBITDA at 0.75x), and generates solid FCF.

But to me, it still feels like the underdog compared to other Grocery/Supermarket picks that check all the same boxes but also come with stronger growth drivers and less macro baggage.

At the end of the day, stability is what you want in this kind of stock. And since a big chunk of North West Company demand is tied to government transfer programs, that stability can start to look a bit shaky.

Since my last article a little under two months ago, the stock is down just over 1%, while the S&P gained a bit more than 3% in the same period.

The stock was sitting a little over $50 before Q2 earnings, which pretty much tells you the market didn't love what it saw.

I think the headwinds (wildfires in Canada, smaller social benefit transfers, and a softer Caribbean market) set the tone more than the positives like the Next 100 program or the private label test.

Fires and Cuts: How Do They Impact Sales?

Back in July, I flagged two major headwinds I expected to weigh on Q2 and beyond:

  1. Wildfires in Manitoba, Ontario and Saskatchewan (14 million acres burned, 500 structures destroyed and 39,000 people evacuated), affecting ~10% of stores near the affected areas;
  2. Cuts to government benefits — from SNAP reductions to federal subsidy freezes. According to Brookings and Alaska Beacon, about 60% of tribes are dealing with frozen benefits, and roughly a quarter have seen them cut altogether.

But let's dig a little deeper here.

Starting with the fires, the CEO mentioned that six more stores than initially estimated were hit, bringing the total to 16 locations affected by the wildfires.

And, despite a sequential reduction in August (primarily in Ontario, with only three locations completely evacuated and others partially), they remain strong in other regions in Saskatchewan, Northwest Territories, and Manitoba (areas with a high concentration of North West Company brands like Giant Tiger, Northern Store, and NorthMart).

I also think that soaring energy costs (2 to 6 times the national average in some regions) made worse by damaged hydroelectric lines, are increasing reliance on diesel and putting a bit of a damper on both discretionary and essential spending.

So basically, it's not just the fires themselves, but their aftermath that could keep putting pressure on The North West Company.

Looking at income transfer programs, there are two that overlap the most with North West Company’s customer base:

  • Jordan's Principle: This program focuses on the monetary and service needs of First Nations children. Demand has grown steadily since 2016, and especially after 2021. However, in February of this year, the federal government made some adjustments to eligibility, making them more restrictive. To give you an idea, the projection for 2026 is that they will be ~$780 million (well below the ~$1.6 billion in 2024).

  • Inuit Child First Initiative, also known as ICFI: This is essentially very similar to Jordan's Principle, but specifically targeted at Inuit children. The most important aspect for The North West Company is the food vouchers, which were extended until the end of March next year, creating some uncertainty about whether they will continue after that. In April, like Jordan's, the ICFI also talked about some changes, making them more restrictive ($167.5 million in 2023-2024, decreasing by 27.3% to $121.7 million for 2025-2026).

Interestingly, both programs were in full swing around 2021, a period during which the company reported double-digit growth in revenue and EBITDA. This is a stark contrast to the slow pace the company has seen over the past five years, with revenue averaging just 4% and EBITDA around 2.7%.

Taking a Look at Q2 FY 2025

Looking at the quarterly results, we can easily see why I called the company 'boring' in my last write-up.

Sales came in at $647 million in Q2—basically flat compared to last year. Considering the headwinds I mentioned, that's not terrible, but it's a clear slowdown from the 3.8% growth we saw in Q1, keeping the deceleration trend going.

Just take a closer look:

There was a 1.8% year-over-year decline in same-store sales in Canada (underperforming the system, which saw same-store sales decline 1.1% year-over-year), offset by stronger sales at NorthMart Pharmacy and air cargo.

Gross margin held steady at 33.9%, basically the same as last year, with just a tiny 0.1% uptick. In other words, whatever boost the 'Next 100' is bringing is being offset, at least for now, by customers spending a bit less per visit.

On the bright side, private labels are rolling out by the end of October—so we could see a nice bump after that.

Due to slightly lower SG&A expenses year over year, EBIT was $55.2 million, an increase of just 0.6% year over year. Net income saw slightly higher growth (+2.2% year over year), landing at $36.1 million, benefiting from lower interest on debt.

Looking at cash flow, they reported OCF of $44.1 million. Due to Capex of $30.8 million, FCF was $13.3 million—slightly exceeding the $13.2 million in the same period last year.

They also issued $15.9 million in net debt, allowing them to continue returning capital to shareholders. Dividends temporarily exceeded OCF ($19.2 million), and they also returned $4.5 million in buybacks, nothing significant.

And no, even though distributions exceeded OCF, I don't see a problem here, since:

  1. Operating asset headwinds reduced OCF by nearly $20 million in the quarter;
  2. They are sitting on $68.5 million in cash. This cushion could protect these returns during periods when OCF is weaker;
  3. Even with net debt in Q2 sitting at $315.4 million in long-term debt, the company still has plenty of breathing room. Debt/EBITDA is right around 1x on a TTM basis, so they could easily take on more if needed.

That said, I continue to project a low single-digit shareholder yield year-over-year even if they slightly accelerate buybacks in H2 FY 2025.

Is North West Company Still 'Hold'?

Since the outlook hasn't really changed much—headwinds still eating into the benefits of the 'Next 100,' tourism in Alaska staying weak, and the South Pacific remaining soft—I'd say my earlier predictions still hold up.

In this case, based on a projected EBITDA of ~$299 million, we would be looking at an EV/EBITDA multiple of 8.8x.

This is a premium compared to peers like Empire Company (OTCPK:EMLAF) (7.5x), SpartanNash (SPTN) (6.9x), and Parkland (PKI:CA) (6.9x). Compared to multinationals like Ahold Delhaize (OTCQX:ADRNY) (6.3x) and Tesco (OTCPK:TSCDY) (7.9x), this stock also appears stretched.

Now, I agree that we should consider the consistency of dividends paid (in the case of North West Company, it has 27 years of consecutive payments), but many names on this list have a high single-digit shareholder yield, easily surpassing North West.

I also think it deserves to trade at a discount to peers like Empire and Parkland, considering its exposure to macro and natural risks—and the fact that it's only now starting to roll out private labels.

And, in fact, it seems the market has been considering this discount for the last five years. Historically, North West is trading at stretched multiples, unlike Parkland, for example.

That said, my rating here remains 'Hold.' I definitely wouldn't pay such a premium to be exposed to these risks.

However, the stock isn't significantly overvalued.

In my view, it's trading just a bit above fair value in the mid-$40s range. Beyond that, it's still the same old boring but steady bond proxy you can lean on in tougher times. Dividends are well covered and should keep coming, so it's a good thing to hold in your portfolio.

data.title: Kadant: There's Potential, Just Not At This Time
data.description:
Company Description

Kadant Inc. (NYSE:KAI) is a manufacturer and supplier of engineered equipment and systems such as fluid handling, filtration, doctoring, material handling, among other things. The company has three reportable segments: Flow Control, Industrial Processing, and Material Handling. Kadant employs approximately 3,500 people, and sports a current market cap of a little under $4B. Over the past decade, KAI has performed well, with a total return of almost 750%, equating to compound annual growth rate of about 23.7%.

Quality Financial Metrics

Let's examine some key quantitative financial metrics for the company and assess how it has performed, and where it might go.

The revenue per share for Kadant has grown by about 2.5x over the past decade, giving it a strong CAGR of 11%. The company experienced back to back years of 20%+ RPS growth in 2017 and 2018, along with another nearly 11% rise in FY19. As with most companies KAI incurred a setback around the pandemic, but followed that up with another 23% gain in RPS. Although the growth has slowed some over the past few years, it is still growing at a respectable rate.

The gross profit margin began the chart above 46%, before dipping to a chart low of 41.7% in FY19. The GPM recovered following that brief decline, and just last year eclipsed the 44% mark for the first time since 2017.

The return on invested capital for KAI started above 11% before declining to just 1.2% in FY17. Since that low point, it has oscillated some, including surpassing 12% in 2023. I typically look for companies with a ROIC at least in the mid-teens, but ideally above 20%...so although the other metrics look solid, Kadant has some work to do in this arena to really grab my attention.

Dividend Data

Kadant has grown its dividend for a little more than a decade, and currently pays a quarterly dividend of $0.34, or $1.36 annually, resulting in a forward dividend yield of just 0.42%. The dividend growth was strong early on but slowed considerably between 2019 and 2022, with an average dividend increase of around 4.2%. However, over the last two years the dividend hikes have been better, with a 9.7% in 2023, and a 10.6% increase last year. Earlier this year the company rewarded shareholders with an increase of 6.3%, and based on the extremely low payout ratio, has plenty of room to continue raising the dividend for the foreseeable future.

Earnings Update

In late July the company announced their Q2 earnings with Non-GAAP EPS of $2.31, $0.37 above the consensus, on revenue of $255M which topped estimates by nearly $10M. Additionally, GAAP EPS surpassed analyst expectations by $0.26 per share, coming in at $2.22.

Starting with the Flow Control segment, revenue increased 4% to $95.9M for the quarter, 2% of that increase was organic, while the remaining amount was related to acquisitions and foreign exchange rates. The results in this segment were due to higher demand for parts and consumables products, with the North American market more than making up for the weakness in the European market. Moving forward management stated in the earnings call they expected demand to continue, and growth to be supported by recurring revenue from their base, along with expansions into new markets through acquisitions (Babbini). As with most worldwide operations, tariff related uncertainty still looms and could constrain customers short-term capital projects.

The Industrial Processing segment struggled mightily, with sales declining 16% compared to Q2 2024. Organically, sales retreated more almost $20M, or 17%, while currency translation had a minimal positive impact. The main catalyst here was weak demand for capital equipment products within their wood processing division. Looking to the future, management expects this segment to have the strongest bookings growth, specifically in wood processing. There is an expectation that there will be a lag between orders and the company being able to recognize revenue, even with shipments increasing for the rest of the year.

And lastly, the Material Handling portion of the business saw sales drop about 6%, from $67M to $63M. Acquisitions contributed a non-material amount, while currency translation partially offset the overall 8% decline in organic revenue. The uncertainty surrounding the tariffs was the root cause here with this segment seeing lower capital equipment revenue. Tariff related impacts are expected to continue for this segment, obviously affecting international sales. Management expects this division to stabilize as opposed to grow even with ongoing challenges from overall market uncertainty.

Leading up to the earnings release shares rose 4%; however, once the earnings came out post-market, shares dropped more than 7% the following day. Since the Q2 earnings call shares have recovered a few points, but are still down more than 5%.

Moving Forward

Kadant has a strong backlog, and management expects demand to continue, and possibly even be better in the second half of the year. Some guidance remains the same with full-year revenue projected to be $1.02B - $1.04B, and adjusted earnings per share between $9.05 and $9.25. However, GAAP EPS is now estimated in the range of $8.89 to $9.09, revised down from $8.97 to $9.17. (The valuation using these numbers and my own valuation method will be discussed in the next section)

The next earnings release is expected to occur in late October and analysts are estimating GAAP EPS of $2.20 per share, with normalized EPS of $2.18, on revenue of $260M. It's worth noting the four analysts that cover Kadant have all revised their EPS estimates down during the last 90 days.

Valuation

The last consideration is whether KAI is trading for an attractive valuation, and to determine this we'll look at the valuation from a free cash flow perspective. Also, to give a little more context on how to view the chart below, the estimated fair value is calculated by dividing the FCF/share for that year by the trailing 5-year average of the FCF yield. The discount line is 10% below the estimated FV, while the premium is 10% above, and finally, the Trim line is 40% above the approximate FV.

My custom free cash flow valuation model suggests Kadant is trading for a good-sized premium, with a current price of about $313 per share and an estimated price closer to $268. KAI saw their FCF/share rise significantly in 2021 following the height of the pandemic, although it returned to its previous level in 2022. However, over the last three years there have been decent gains in the FCF per share, as indicated by the higher valuation zones, and the stock has followed. Looking ahead, the stock is currently trading for a P/E ratio of more than 35, while its 5-year average, according to Seeking Alpha, is less than 28. This indicates an overvaluation of approximately 25%, which is higher than my valuation model's estimate of about 17% currently, (based on the current share price and my aforementioned estimated price of $268).

Using management's guidance of $9.15 at the mid point, and the current and 5-year average P/E Ratio, we can estimate a low point of about $256 (28 * $9.15) while the high point is approximately $320 (35*$9.15), which is only slightly above the current share price of $313.

The projected long-term rate of return for KAI is rather weak at just 5.03% with the pieces of that estimate being a forward dividend yield of 0.43%, a return to fair value factor of -3.19%, and an estimated earnings growth rate of 7.79%.

Final Thoughts

Kadant has outperformed the overall market considerably over the past decade, with average gains of more than 20% annually. The financials metrics are fairly strong with some work to be done on the ROIC. The dividend history is encouraging with more than a decade of dividend growth, coupled with a low payout ratio. The most recent earnings report was mixed as it saw only one segment with a rise in revenue, while the other two had declines, one significantly. Management seems optimistic about the rest of the year, but I'm not quite sold, I want to see what Q3 brings first. Ultimately, I think KAI should be rated a HOLD as the valuation appears expensive, and the Q2 earnings results weren't stellar. Kadant is a company I'd like to own at some point, but it needs to be at the right price, and although the price has come down over the past week, it's just not there at this time.

data.title: CSW Industrials: Hidden Factors Overlooked By The Market
data.description:
I rate CSW Industrials (NYSE:CSW) as a buy, as I notice that the market is overlooking important long-term drivers that might boost the stock price over the years significantly. I should acknowledge that something that surprised me about this business, was the combination of its mid-cap size and its business quality, and even better, a well-managed business by a competent and well-incentivized CEO. From my experience with another mid-cap I assessed months ago, Nemetschek, I know that mid-size companies with a long-term orientation serving different niche segments in a very fragmented market tend to be long-term winners. Let's see if this company fulfills the latter.

Let's go.

Context

In Q1 FY 2026, CSW grew 16.5% YoY, missing the expectations slightly by 4.8%, whereas GAAP EPS reported $2.43, declining 1.6% YoY as a result of the increased share count, which was a result of the equity offering in September 2024 to fund the Aspen Acquisition. However, the non-GAAP EPS, which offers a clearer view of operating performance, grew 2.5% YoY in the same period.

In regard to FY 2025, revenue growth was 10.8% YoY, reaching a record $878.3 million. This growth can be explained by organic growth of 4.8% and by inorganic growth of 6%. Adjusted EPS diluted grew 20% YoY, so revenue and earnings both showed growth. Now, we need to understand briefly which kind of products CSW sells to get an overall picture about its long-term tailwinds.

Business model

CSW is a well-diversified industrial growth company particularly focused on offering niche value-added products through three different business segments: Contractor Solutions (CS), Specialized Reliability Solutions (SRS), and Engineered Building Solutions (EBS).

In the chart above from July 2025, we see that CS is the most important segment by far, with more than 70% of the total CSW's revenues, making it critical to understand the CSW's long-term prospects. This segment offers innovative products mainly for residential and commercial HVAC/R and plumbing applications, electrical and firestopping consumables/accessories, and GRDs (grilles/registers/diffusers), and, recently, evaporator coils & air handlers with the recent acquisition of Aspen.

Clients in this segment are wholesalers, OEMs, and indirectly HVAC contractors/installers; the market is highly fragmented, giving CSW pricing power and resilience while reducing the customer concentration risk. In general, contractors prefer branded, reliable accessories, generating a sticky demand. Conversely, distributors want a broad and reliable product line through a one-stop shop, whereas OEMs are more focused on long-term relationships through more value, quality, and compliance.

CSW fits very well in each type of client's preference, which contributes to solidifying its market position and competitive advantages. In this segment, CSW offers 16 different groups of products through 28 different brands.

The second segment is the SRS, which contributes around 16% of the CSW's total revenues. This segment delivers products that protect assets in the most demanding environments and extreme conditions and solve equipment maintenance challenges. Products like specialty lubricants, compounds, sealants, desiccant breather filtration products, and lubrication management systems are demanded by operators and OEMs in heavy/continuous-duty industries that care about uptime and asset life.

This segment serves the mining & rail sector, energy/oil & gas, power, and general industrial sectors. In general, these sectors are moderately to highly fragmented, composed of a few integrated majors as well as many niche players.

Finally, the EBS, which contributes around 13% of the CSW's total revenues. This segment offers mainly code-driven, life-safety products that are engineered to provide aesthetically pleasing solutions for the construction, refurbishment, and modernization of commercial, institutional, and multi-family residential buildings. Customers are general contractors, architects, and institutional/commercial builders in a highly fragmented market.

Summarizing the three segments, CS is the largest, most resilient, and strongest moat; then, the SRS is a more niche, cyclical, industrial-focused growth segment below that of CS's segment. Last but not least, the EBS is the smallest, fragmented, projected-based growth, which is linked to construction.

In the table above we can see the evolution of the CSW's sales in the last 11 fiscal years, producing a CAGR of around 12%.

CSW's financial performance

I collected some data from the CSW's 10k from the last nine years, and this is what I've found:

In general, CSW has shown a very decent performance over the long haul; in 2017 and 2018, CSW was in the middle of a restructuring such as manufacturing consolidation, procurement efficiencies, and aligning cost structure toward more growth. All this took place after the spin-off from Capital Southwest (CSWC) in 2015.

That's why we see low ROE in 2017 and 2018, but still the FCF was positive. In general, the company has shown decent growth over the years, even in the COVID-19 scenario when most businesses that offer physical products found serious difficulties in those years to sell their products. Gross margins have been kept over 44% consistently, with low debt levels, FCF margins of more than 18%, particularly in the last 2 years, and a consistent share buyback program.

For a business with a market cap of around $4.4 billion, it is rare to find these good metrics, as I've seen more similar metrics in "large caps." In this sense, we need to understand the qualitative aspects behind the CSW's financial metrics to see if there are some kinds of risks over the long haul mostly related to disruption or competition.

Competitive advantages

I've found that CSW was holding around 184 patents globally with information updated in 2023. According to CSW's 10k, these patents can last between 10 and 20 years, so there is room to keep developing new patents as the older ones are ending. In my view, this is a clear indication that CSW protects its innovations, putting a barrier to competitors to imitate those innovations.

In addition, patents give CSW more pricing power as customers find it difficult to encounter possible substitutes for those products. Another interesting advantage of CSW is its supply chain efficiency as a result of its long-term strategic relationships spanning 17 years, strong global vendor network, and advanced procurement systems. These factors are very important to ensure timely delivery and cost-effective operations, reinforcing the loyalty toward the CSW's brands.

I can infer that the company uses lean manufacturing and advanced technologies to optimize production, reducing costs and enhancing productivity. Now, I haven't found information that indicates explicitly when CSW started using those advanced processes, but I know that in 2022, CSW was focused on capacity expansion, continuous improvement, and automation to increase productivity; these three terms are often part of the "lean manufacturing process."

Thus, in the table above of the CSW's metrics, we can see a material improvement in the FCF margins since 2022 as a result of the CSW's strategic changes to be more efficient. All these qualitative aspects give me confidence that those high FCF margins in the last years will be kept, the revenue growth will be sustainable, and the returns on capital, ROE, and ROIC will be both higher than 12% at low debt levels.

Main long-term drivers and interest rate cuts impact in CSW's business

There are several drivers that might boost the CSW's stock in the upcoming years; I could identify first those that are easier to spot. Indeed, it's highly likely that there will be higher demand for energy efficiency & HVAC as buildings account for around 40% of the global energy use while regulations push for higher HVAC efficiency, electrification, and IAQ systems.

The global HVAC system is expected to grow at a CAGR of 6.4% from 2025 to 2030, and this market is attended by CSW's CS segment, as we've seen before, representing more than 70% of CSW's total revenues; this is clearly the steadiest and largest growth driver. The other driver is the urgent need for more urbanization & infrastructure modernization.

Indeed, an aging US, global infrastructure, and urban densification were the motivation to launch the $1.2 trillion Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA), which stimulates sustained public capital expenditure over decades, benefiting demand for firestopping, sealants, railings, and other building products. This driver will benefit the CS and EBS segments of CSW in the upcoming years.

A third driver that I identified is safety & regulatory compliance, as I've found information about the EU pushing for more energy efficiency in commercial buildings such as offices and hospitals. On the other hand, green building practices are becoming mainstream, driven by emissions regulations and net-zero commitments. Firestopping and safety compliance needs are increasing globally, particularly in high-density urban environments.

On the other hand, builders are moving from commoditized to performance-based materials, in which CSW excels with patented offerings.

A fourth driver is the reshoring, nearshoring, and supply chain security, a trend that is a priority for the Trump administration. This would mean higher demand for industrial assets such as lubricants, friction modifiers, reliability systems, etc.

Interest rate cuts

There are important signs that we might be getting into an interest rate cuts scenario given the recent report of a softening labor market, elevated unemployment rates, the strong market expectations about interest rate cuts, and the Fed's officials' views about it. As such, in my view, it's very important to include this driver in any bullish case to understand if this potential catalyst is significant or not in each particular case.

Now, it's reasonable to think that lower interest rates can reduce the financing costs for construction projects, boosting the expenses in infrastructure and construction. In addition, lower interest rates improve the cash liquidity position of companies in the CSW's industry, so their capacity to invest in equipment increases too. These impacts clearly benefit the CSW's CS segment, which represents around 70% of the CSW's total revenues.

The CSW's SRS segment also benefits from lower interest rates, as the demand in other sectors such as energy or mining can be boosted too, where CSW has key customers who may increase the demand for specialty lubricants, compounds, sealants, etc. Even the CSW's EBS will benefit too, as the real estate market and construction will be more stimulated under interest rate cuts.

"Hidden" drivers for the long haul

Now, I could notice that CSW has a very good history of acquisitions since its spin-off in 2015:

In this sense, CSW has completed 17 acquisitions, including the last one, Aspen Manufacturing in May 2025, which meant a total accumulated capital deployed of around $1 billion and an average EBITDA multiple paid for its acquisitions of around 8x. Now, we need to make sure that acquisitions will continue being part of CSW's strategic plans in the upcoming years.

As such, it would be important to know if there are plenty of opportunities to keep buying more subsidiaries that fit well in CSW's business model. I've found that there is evidence of hundreds of small, family-owned or regional firms across HVAC components such as grilles, diffusers, ducting, coils, etc., lacking the distribution scale or capital required to meet energy, climate, or efficiency requirements facing succession pressures. Thus, there are lots of these natural sellers that might fit with CSW's requirements to be potential targets.

Now, going a bit deeper, I noticed that Private Equity (PE) firms are very active in HVAC or specialty service companies in the CSW's industry, which might be a problem for the latter, as I know that PEs are willing to pay higher multiples, so there could be an argument in favor of PE buying many potential targets of CSW.

However, it's clear that the strategies of CSW and PEs are very different even when both target a similar range of sizes of potential subsidiaries in the same sectors. Indeed, CSW focuses on smaller tuck-in acquisitions that complement its current portfolio. CSW is way more selective than a PE firm, as the latter can buy a wider range of companies with the only goal of taking them over to unlock value and then selling them after an average period of 5 years.

Furthermore, PE firms used to apply a leveraged buyout strategy to buy their targets, filling their balance sheets with new debt; as a result, owners see that cash that could have been used for more R&D or digitalization ends up going to debt service. In addition, family-owned businesses are reluctant to lose autonomy as PEs take over, applying their usual playbook. So, I think that PEs are not a real problem for CSW to pursue more and more acquisitions in the upcoming years.

The main evidence that this is not a problem for CSW is the data I showed before, that CSW acquired 17 subsidiaries since 2015 with an average EBITDA multiple of 8x, which indicates that CSW has not overpaid on average as a result of the competition with PE firms.

Last but not least, we should not forget the potential effect of the interest rate cuts on the CSW's M&A strategy, as, under that scenario, acquisition financing becomes cheaper, and CSW can pursue larger and more frequent acquisitions while keeping discipline. PE firms also benefit from this scenario, but CSW's reputation for preserving brands while offering synergies makes it the preferred buyer for many family-owned businesses.

Skin in the game of the CEO is another "hidden" driver

I like to see if the management is adequately compensated to perform at his best for the long haul, particularly after a spinoff happens. In CSW's case, in 2015, there were several motivations to spin CSW off from CSWC, and one of them was to unlock value in both companies.

CEO Armes holds 24.2x his base salary in CSW stock, which exceeds the 6x guidelines. This clearly shows a massive personal alignment with shareholder value. Mr. Armes holds 0.4% of CSW shares, which are estimated to be $18 million in fair market value as of mid-2025. In my view, this is very important, as it is in the CEO's best interest to deliver long-term value for shareholders.

In FY2025, CEO Armes earned $6.33 million, of which 86% of those earnings were equity-based compensation through stock awards and performance. For the 2023-2025 period, CSW's CEO received a performance-based long-term incentive, a TSR-linked one, at 200% of the target, reflecting the 95th percentile performance relative to the Russell 2000.

A TSR, total shareholder return, only rises when the CEO makes acquisitions that truly create value, not just grow size. Also, TSR rises when the CEO implements a more disciplined capital allocation while driving operational improvements.

Valuation

According to SA, CSW scores "D-," showing that all the valuation multiples are higher than its comparables. However, we need to consider that all these metrics are compared with companies that are not within the niche sectors where CSW operates.

I propose the discounted cash flow method (DCF), so I will make some assumptions:

  • Outstanding shares: 16.3 million

  • FCF margins: 16.5% (considering the changes made by CSW to improve its operational processes in 2022 and 2023)

  • Revenue growth: I took the revenue projections 2026-2028 from SA; for 2029 and 2030, I picked values considering a CAGR of around 9% from 2025 to 2030, considering that the CSW's revenues grew at 12% CAGR historically, as mentioned before.

  • Cash as of June 2025: $38 million.

  • Debt as of June 2025: $95 million.

  • Discounted rate: 8% (I considered CSW a very good business that might be getting into an interest rate cuts scenario.)

  • FCF growth in perpetuity: 3.8% annually (considering that the FCF grew at 18% CAGR from 2018 to 2025).

To find the perpetuity, we used the formula:

Perpetuity = FCF 2031/(discounted rate - g), where g = FCF growth in perpetuity, which was assumed to be 3.8% annually.

With perpetuity, we calculate the present value of all the FCFs beyond 2030. Then, we calculate the enterprise value using the following:

Enterprise Value = Present Value of FCF (from 2026 to 2030) + Perpetuity + Cash - Total Debt.

Finally, the intrinsic value is calculated by taking the enterprise value and dividing it by the outstanding number of shares. I got an intrinsic value of around $339 per share, and the current market price is around $256, which would mean an upside of 32%. Of course, I will not say that the stock is cheap now, but after a fall of 40% from its peak in November 2024, I would not consider it expensive either.

Now, I would not consider CSW as a short-term trade, as I've explained before; there are interesting long-term drivers that might boost the stock price consistently in the upcoming years. As such, the strategy that I was using was to accumulate shares gradually, waiting for all those catalysts to materialize. In my view, the best strategy with this stock is to buy at decent prices to accumulate shares for a "buy & hold" strategy.

I like to see other valuation metrics such as the total enterprise value/EBIT, in which CSW is currently trading at 23.8x, whereas its average of the last 5 years is 25.9x. Another metric is the price/diluted EPS (PER GAAP), which is currently trading at 30.19x, whereas the average of the last 5 years is 35.3x. So, it does not mean the stock is a bargain but is not expensive either; I would consider it a decent price to start accumulating shares for the long haul.

Risks

An important risk is that CSW grows at a slower pace than expected or that CSW might not find suitable acquisitions in the next 5 years to support its long-term growth. Fortunately, there are plenty of potential targets within the CSW's sectors, as we've seen before, to keep making acquisitions that complement even more the CSW's portfolio of products.

Another risk is a scenario where more tariffs are imposed as part of the Trump administration's strategy. As such, it's important where CSW's manufacturing operations are located; this is what I've found in the last 10k:

Our manufacturing operations are concentrated in the United States (“U.S.”), Vietnam and Canada, and we have distribution operations in the U.S., Australia, Canada and the United Kingdom (“U.K.”). Our products are sold directly to end-users or through designated channels in over 100 countries around the world, primarily in the U.S., Canada, the U.K. and Australia.

Indeed, there are parts of the production that come from outside the US, so that could raise input costs, reducing margins. In the last call, the management mentioned the strategy to reduce the tariffs' impact on the business, such as stockpiling inventory ahead of tariff implementation, relocating manufacturing locations away from higher tariff regions, leveraging Vietnam operations, and implementing specific pricing actions assessing product by product to protect margins.

So, it's clear that the management is vigilant, actively working to manage exposure through operational and geographic adjustments. In this sense, the CSW's pricing power and the management's ability represent a very good margin of safety for a long-term investor in this company.

Another risk might be the competition, as there are big players that could represent a long-term threat to CSW. Indeed, companies like 3M (MMM), which sells industrial adhesives, tapes, and coatings; Carlisle Companies (CSL), which offers roofing and specialty materials; or Parker-Hannifin (PH), which offers engineered materials and industrial systems, are way larger than CSW in terms of market cap and size.

Nevertheless, even when these big players are well-diversified companies with global scale, R&D resources, and distribution networks, CSW has developed a niche focus in high-margin and less commoditized markets, particularly in HVAC/R, firestopping, and specialty coatings. As a result, by narrowing its focus, CSW sidesteps direct competition with diversified industrial leaders.

Takeaways

I think that CSW is a very interesting case of a high-quality mid-cap with the potential to keep growing over the long haul with a well-incentivized management that will work hard to deliver the maximum value. I like the business very much, as it has relatively low disruption risk, as it protects its products with patents that last between 10 and 20 years, which gives it enough time to keep developing new innovations to protect in the future.

What really called my attention about this company is that I've found a mid-cap with such quality in its business model, and I know that high-quality and well-managed mid-caps tend to perform very well over the long haul, particularly if they cover niche segments that enable them to keep growing sustainably over the long haul through new acquisitions, organic growth, and pricing power. CSW fits really well under that small group of companies.

data.title: Global Monetary Base - Update Through August 2025 And 2025 Forecast
data.description:
The team at Infrastructure Capital Advisors tracks the Global Monetary Base, defined as currency in circulation plus bank reserves, as an indicator of current and future interest rates. This article provides data and insights through August 2025, with data released in September 2025.

Global Monetary Base (GMB):

In August, GMB fell by 0.4% MoM and has fallen by $194 billion, or 0.8%, over the last twelve months. Inflation is caused by excessive monetary growth, but the money supply has decreased by 3.7% over the last three years. We expect that the GMB will stabilize over the next quarter and start growing in 1H’26 as the European Central Bank (ECB) and the People's Bank of China (PBOC) are forced to ease monetary policy in response to slower global growth. Recent economic data show that inflation continues to steadily moderate with recent CPI and PCE core coming in at 0.3%.

If the Global Monetary Base remains flat through 2025, we expect interest rates to stabilize and decline as central banks cut rates in response to slow or negative growth. Declining global interest rates and a flat to increasing GMB will support the global stock and bond markets.

data.title: Don't Rush To Buy Uber (Downgrade)
data.description:
It has been a while since I have covered Uber Technologies, Inc. (NYSE:UBER)(NEOE:UBER:CA). Back in the second half of February, I initiated the stock at a buy rating and expressed my view that investors should follow Bill Ackman's lead. In that analysis, I found healthy growth and a cheap valuation, and since then, the stock has advanced around 16%. In the below analysis, it is shown that Q2 was a respectable quarter, but for Q3, they are expecting gross bookings growth to be relatively unchanged, and there may be some margin pressure. With that being the case, the forward EV/EBITDA ratio may have some room for a pullback after significant expansion. However, when considering that the company is pushing into new areas of transportation, such as robotaxis, the long term outlook for Uber still seems to be bright. Therefore, as a result of a balancing act between near term pressures but long term potential, I have decided to downgrade the stock to just a hold.

Business Activity

Let's start off today's analysis with a look at their user metrics. In their Q2, MAPCs increased by 15% to 180 million. This shows that the company continues to attract new customers to their platform. As you can see above, this is the strongest growth in the past five quarters and so there are acceleration trends for this figure. However, for monthly trips per MAPC, growth decelerated. For Q2, they reported 6.1 trips per MAPC, representing 2% growth. While this shows that customer demand for their services was resilient, the continued slowdown in growth may be a cause for some caution. The slower growth may be a reflection of weaker consumer sentiment. Nonetheless, with MAPC growth being robust, total trips saw an 18% increase in Q2, unchanged from the growth rate in previous two quarters. Therefore, overall business activity for the company has held strong despite a tough macro environment. Lastly, gross bookings aren't shown in the stats above but they also saw 18% in constant currency growth, unchanged from Q1. As a whole, there aren't many indications that demand is slowing down at this point.

Top Line Results

Uber reported their 2025 Q2 results in early August and top line figures were resilient. The generated total revenues of $12.65 billion, up 18% YoY in reported terms and in constant currency. While this growth rate isn't particularly high when compared to late 2022, there was notable acceleration from the previous quarter. Do note that in constant currency terms, Q1 growth was actually 17% and so Q2 is only a modest improvement. Still, the company was able to easily surpass analyst expectations as they beat by a rather impressive $185.83 million to show that business activity has been strong in dollar terms as well. In terms of their segments, their flagship Mobility business saw some growth deceleration for their gross bookings and revenue growth is notably lower than in the prior year period. On the other hand, their Deliveries business saw both gross bookings and revenue growth accelerate. Therefore, the role of the growth engine may be shifting to the latter.

Recent Announcements

From the company's recent announcements of partnerships and investments, it should be clear that they are not static. On the contrary, Uber is positioning itself to be relevant as new technologies in transportation advance. Let's briefly discuss a few developments that show the company is positioning itself for future growth.

On September 4th, it was reported that Uber's investment into Lucid's (LCID) robotaxi program had closed. The investment was previously announced in July and later next year, the robotaxi service is expected to launch in a major U.S. city. Therefore, instead of seeing autonomous vehicles as a potential threat to the business, Uber is embracing these new technological advancements. This is a strategic investment to make sure that the company remains relevant. As another indication that Uber is aggressively positioning itself for the robotaxi era, it was previous announced in May that they would partner with China's Momenta. On September 8th, it was announced that they will begin testing in Munich in 2026. Therefore, Uber's autonomous push is global.

In addition to vehicles on the ground, the company seems to be now pushing into the air mobility services space as well. It was announced just days ago that Joby's (JOBY) Blade services will soon be available for booking on the Uber app. Joby acquired the passenger business back in August and now it is reported that customers will be able to book flights directly on the Uber app as early as next year. With air mobility already gaining significant traction in international markets like China, Uber's push into this space is another highly strategic one which could payoff down the road.

From these recent announcements, I believe it is clear that Uber is actively mitigating disruption risk. If the company were to solely focus on just improving their existing business, investors could easily see robotaxis or air mobility as future risks. Instead, through the above partnerships and investments, the company has positioned itself as a potential beneficiary of new transportation advancements. Therefore, management does deserve applause for being nimble and forward-looking.

Cash Flows & Buybacks

For the company as a whole, adjusted EBITDA increased by 35% YoY to $2.119 billion. That shows quite strong profitability but their cash flow metrics look even better. For Q2, operating cash flows increased by 40.88% to $2.564 billion. With this outpacing EBITDA gains by a notable amount, investors should credit Uber with strong cash generation. After subtracting the cash used for purchases of property and equipment, FCF is calculated to be $2.475 billion. As shown in the above chart, this represents growth of 43.81%. While there is a deceleration trend in FCF growth, this figure for Q2 should nonetheless be encouraging for investors that are seeking capital returns. Speaking of capital returns, Uber upped share buybacks from $325 million to $1.363 billion YoY for Q2. That is a major increase and shows that the company is becoming more willing to return capital to shareholders. In addition, on the day of their Q2 earnings, the company announced that they approved a new $20 billion share repurchase authorization. From these developments, I believe it is clear that the FCF strength is a huge positive for investors.

Q3 Guidance

Uber provided Q3 guidance for a few metrics. For gross bookings, they are now projecting a range of $48.25 billion to $49.75 billion. This represents a constant currency growth range of 17% to 21% YoY or 16% to 20% when excluding the Trendyol Go acquisition. Recall that in Q2, gross bookings increased 18% in constant currency and so overall, Q3 is expected to be more of the same in terms of overall demand. As for adjusted EBITDA, Uber is projecting a range of $2.19 billion to $2.29 billion. They state that this represents a growth range of 30% to 36% which would be a notable deceleration at the midpoint compared to Q2's 35% increase. Therefore, while demand is expected to remain strong, there may be some modest profitability pressures moving forward.

Valuation Around Fair

Uber's forward EV/EBITDA ratio has expanded significantly from the lows of earlier this year. At the current reading of 23.38, I believe there are reasons to be somewhat cautious. As a whole, Q2 was a respectable quarter. While there was some weakness in growth for their mobility segment, the strength in delivery and overall profitability enhancements for the company showed that the fundamentals are strong. However, in the above guidance discussion, it is shown that there will be some margin pressures moving forward even if demand growth remains resilient. Therefore, the forward EV/EBITDA may have some room for a pullback in the near future. That said, Seeking Alpha rates the stock's valuation as a C- as of this writing. From the above discussion regarding the company's push into robotaxis and air mobility, the conclusion was that the company is strategically positioning itself well. Therefore, with the long term outlook of the company still looking bright, the stock is likely fairly valued as long term opportunity is balanced with some near term pressures.

Technical Analysis

For the long term, the technicals remain quite solid. The 50 day SMA has been above the 200-day SMA for the past 6 months to show sustained bullishness, and the deviation between the lines remains large enough for comfort. As for the near term, the 9 day EMA recently bounced off the 21 day EMA as a signal of resilient bulls. The gap between the EMAs is starting to expand again as momentum seems to be picking back up. Furthermore, the stock has also just reclaimed the Bollinger Bands midline. That shows the bulls may be back in control now. In the MACD and RSI indicators, there are positive near-term signals, but as you can see above, there may be negative divergence fears. The MACD is currently on the cusp of a bullish crossover, and the RSI has regained the 50 level to prove that the bulls are indeed in control. However, when compared to the peak in May, both indicators made lower highs in July and then in August. Therefore, the stock may be prone to a significant pullback moving forward.

Conclusion

As stated in the introduction, since my buy rating, Uber stock has advanced by about 16%. After today's analysis, I believe there are good reasons to turn a bit cautious on the stock, at least for the near term. As mentioned above, Q2 was overall a good quarter, but the continued sluggishness in their flagship segment isn't a great sign. More importantly, their guidance shows it's not all smooth sailing moving forward, and so the EV/EBITDA ratio that has expanded significantly from the start of the year may have to take a breather. After considering the long term potential of the company, I would be hesitant to call it overvalued, but in the near term, the likelihood of continued stock outperformance isn't strong. As a result, I believe a downgrade to a hold rating is most appropriate currently.

data.title: Surging Jobless Claims And In Line CPI Validate Our Bullish Rate Outlook
data.description:
The 10-year treasury yield dipped below 4% briefly after jobless claims came in at 263,000, which is the highest level since the end of the pandemic in late 2021. Jobless claims are a leading indicator of the labor market, whereas the actual monthly employment report is a lagging indicator, particularly given a trend of large negative revisions. CPI Core printed in line at .3, with the data series continuing to be distorted by the archaic and arbitrary calculation of shelter, with Owner’s Equivalent Rent inexplicable rising to .4% and 4% Y/Y with actual market rents approximately flat. CPI-R core, which utilizes real time data to estimate shelter vs. the two-year delayed data used in CPI-R, is running at only 1.7% (see figure below). Volatile components of CPI caused the core rate to be elevated, with airline fares rising 5.9%, used cars up 1% and motor vehicle maintenance up 2.4%.

CPI-R continues to be a leading indicator for CPI-U

We continue to be bullish on 10-year treasuries with a 3.5-4.0% year-end target based on the fact that the interest-sensitive sectors of the US economy, housing and construction, are in recession, which is impacting the job market (see figure below). This weakening of the job market will force the Fed to cut 3 times this year and reach the neutral rate of 2.75-3.0% in the first half of 2026. The US 10-year is 70% correlated with the expected terminal rate of Fed Funds, which is currently 3.00%, with a normal spread being 1.05% (see figure below). We are also bullish on stocks as Fed rate cuts and momentum in AI drive stock prices, with our upside target on the S&P at 7,000, which represents 23x our 2026 S&P earnings estimate.

Market implied policy rate continues to forecast 10-year yields
